one_line_takeaway: In Stable Diffusion 3's three-encoder pipeline, the minimal set of text encoders an attacker must poison to reliably backdoor the model depends on the attack's semantic target (single CLIP encoder suffices for object swaps, both CLIP encoders for style/action, all three including T5-XXL for full prompt override), and the proposed MELT method reaches this same attack success using LoRA adapters on under 0.2% of encoder parameters instead of full fine-tuning.
key_finding: The minimal effective encoder subset is target-dependent: TPA (full content override) requires all three encoders (ASR 48/39/27% for single encoders L/G/T5, rising to 70% for L+G, 82% for L+T5, 87% for G+T5, and 98% for L+G+T5 -- T5-XXL plays an outsized role). TOA (object replacement) is achievable by poisoning CLIP-G alone (100% ASR, matching the full three-encoder attack's 100%). TSA (style injection) and TAA (action manipulation) are achievable by poisoning both CLIP encoders (L+G) without T5 (TSA: 100% ASR vs 100% for all three; TAA: 76% ASR vs 76% for all three, the hardest target overall). CLIP_poison scores track the ASR trends across all four targets. Restricting the attack to the minimal subset does not measurably degrade clean-prompt CLIP scores (13.8-15.8, vs. 15.03 clean baseline) or FID relative to attacking all encoders. On top of this, MELT (LoRA on the minimal subset only) matches or slightly exceeds Full Fine-tuning and ME-Rickrolling on ASR/CLIP_poison/CLIP_clean/FID while training far fewer parameters: TPA 99% ASR with 11.40M params (0.2% of the 5583.96M full fine-tuning parameter count); TOA 99% ASR with 6.32M params (0.11% of full fine-tuning, 0.91% of ME-Rickrolling's 695M for the G-only subset); TSA 100% ASR with 8.06M params (0.14% of full fine-tuning); TAA 77% ASR with the same 8.06M params (0.14%). MELT also generalizes to SD 1.5 and SDXL, matching or beating Rickrolling, EvilEdit, and Personalization-based baselines there while using the fewest trainable parameters (e.g., SDXL TOA: MELT 100% ASR with 8.06M params vs. Rickrolling's 98% ASR with 818M params).
core_contribution: The paper presents the first systematic study of text-encoder-based backdoor attacks on multi-encoder text-to-image diffusion models, using Stable Diffusion 3 (SD3, three text encoders: CLIP-L, CLIP-G, T5-XXL) as the testbed. It defines four backdoor attack target types spanning different semantic granularities -- Target Prompt Attack (TPA, full content override), Target Object Attack (TOA, object replacement), Target Style Attack (TSA, style injection), and Target Action Attack (TAA, relational/interaction manipulation) -- and, for each, empirically identifies the minimal subset of the three text encoders that must be poisoned to match the attack success of poisoning all encoders. Building on this, the authors propose MELT (Multi-Encoder Lightweight aTtacks), which injects the backdoor into only the identified minimal encoder subset using LoRA adapters (trained per-encoder with a backdoor loss encouraging student-encoder embeddings of triggered prompts to match teacher embeddings of a target prompt, plus a utility loss to preserve clean-prompt behavior) rather than full fine-tuning of encoder weights.
threat_model: White-box adversary with fine-tuning access to one or more of a diffusion pipeline's text encoders but no access to the rest of the pipeline (diffusion backbone, VAE) and no access to the original pretraining data of those encoders. The attacker builds a poisoned prompt corpus (containing a hidden trigger -- a Latin 'o' visually substituted with a Cyrillic 'o') paired with target outputs and fine-tunes the chosen encoder(s) offline; at inference the attacker cannot modify the deployed model but can insert the trigger into a prompt to activate the backdoor. The paper motivates this as realistic because users copy/paste prompts from untrusted sources and automated prompt-enhancement tools can silently inject such trigger tokens. Attacker goals are (1) reliably produce attacker-chosen content when the trigger is present and (2) preserve normal generation quality when it is absent (stealth/utility preservation).
limitations: The paper explicitly notes: (1) no direct method-level baseline exists on SD3 because prior backdoor methods (e.g., TWIST) were not evaluated beyond two-encoder models and TWIST's implementation is not public, so comparisons on SD3 are against internally constructed Full Fine-tuning/ME-Rickrolling baselines rather than external prior work. (2) TAA (action-level manipulation) never reaches high ASR even when all three encoders are attacked (peaks at 76-77%), which the authors attribute to relational/spatial semantics being only partially controlled by text embeddings and further shaped by the diffusion model's own visual generation process -- i.e., a ceiling the text-encoder attack surface cannot fully overcome. (3) The threat model assumes white-box fine-tuning access to text encoders and relies on a visually similar trigger substitution (Latin/Cyrillic 'o') rather than testing broader trigger designs or defenses/detection against such triggers. (4) Ethical considerations section (Appendix C.4) acknowledges dual-use risk that MELT could be adapted for real-world misuse.

datasets_used: LAION-Aesthetics v2 6.5+ (text prompts for fine-tuning text encoders)
MSCOCO 2014 validation split (50 captions for TPA/TSA attack-success evaluation; 10,000 captions for FID; 50 captions for clean CLIP evaluation)
MSCOCO 2014 training split (used to build classifier training data for TSA/TOA CLIP ViT-B/32 evaluators)
ChatGPT-generated object-centric and action-centric prompt sets (for TOA and TAA evaluation, since MSCOCO captions were insufficiently object/relation-specific)
quantitative_results: TPA ASR by encoder subset: L 48%, G 39%, T5 27%, L+G 70%, L+T5 82%, G+T5 87%, L+G+T5 98%
TSA ASR by encoder subset: L 63%, G 95%, T5 17%, L+G 100%, L+T5 64%, G+T5 97%, L+G+T5 100%
TOA ASR by encoder subset: L 92%, G 100%, T5 58%, L+G 100%, L+T5 96%, G+T5 100%, L+G+T5 100%
TAA ASR by encoder subset: L 37%, G 67%, T5 40%, L+G 76%, L+T5 70%, G+T5 69%, L+G+T5 76%

MELT on SD3 (Table 1): TPA 99% ASR / 30.36 CLIP_poison / 15.34 CLIP_clean / 25.01 FID with 11.40M params (0.2% of 5583.96M full fine-tuning); TOA 99% ASR / 6.32M params (0.11% of full fine-tuning, 0.91% of ME-Rickrolling's 695M); TSA 100% ASR / 8.06M params (0.14% of full fine-tuning); TAA 77% ASR / 8.06M params (0.14% of full fine-tuning)
Clean model baselines (Table 1): CLIP_clean 15.03, FID 24.46 across all targets when unattacked
SD3 utility check (Table 3): CLIP_clean ranges 13.84-15.83 and FID ranges 24.29-26.04 across all encoder-subset/target combinations, close to clean baseline (15.03 / 24.46)
Generalization to SDXL, TOA target (Table 4): MELT 100% ASR with 8.06M params vs. Rickrolling 98% ASR with 818M params, EvilEdit 46% ASR with 19.3M params, Personalization 6% ASR with 860M params
Generalization to SD1.5, TOA target (Table 4): MELT 100% ASR with 1.74M params vs. Rickrolling 99% ASR with 123M params, EvilEdit 100% ASR with 19.3M params, Personalization 92% ASR with 860M params
baselines_compared: Full Fine-tuning (all three SD3 text encoders fully fine-tuned) -- internal baseline constructed by the authors
ME-Rickrolling (Rickrolling-style full fine-tuning restricted to the minimal effective encoder subset identified in the paper's own analysis) -- internal baseline
Rickrolling (Struppek et al., 2023) -- external prior method, used as baseline on SD1.5 and SDXL generalization experiments
Personalization-based backdoor (Huang et al., 2024) -- external baseline on SD1.5/SDXL generalization
EvilEdit (Wang et al., 2024) -- external baseline on SD1.5/SDXL generalization
TWIST (Li et al., 2025) discussed but explicitly NOT used as a direct baseline on SD3 because its implementation is not publicly available and it was only tested on models with at most two encoders
